summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the best programmers combine two counterintuitive traits: laziness and intellectual humility. Lazy programmers build tools to automate tedious work, while 'dumb' programmers maintain a beginner's mind that keeps them learning and asking basic questions. He observes that many developers avoid asking simple questions due to programming culture's macho attitude, yet the best developers he's worked with aren't afraid to look foolish. The post extends this philosophy beyond individual coding to business strategy: successful software acknowledges that users, like developers, take the path of least resistance.
key_points: Lazy programmers are valuable because they build automation tools to avoid repetitive work
Programmers who think they're smart stop learning and become uncritical of their own work
Asking 'dumb' questions is a hallmark of the best developers, not a weakness
The macho programming culture discourages asking basic questions, which hurts code quality
Understanding that people take the path of least resistance is key to building successful software
money_quotes: Nobody is really smart enough to program computers.
In the endless battle between programmer and compiler, give up early and admit that it's always you and never the compiler who is at fault.
Having the guts to ask basic questions is, ironically enough, a hallmark of the best developers I've ever worked with.
Programmers who know they are smart stop learning, stop being critical towards their own work.
The only way you'll ever succeed as a software developer is through humility and the Zen concept of beginner's mind.
Being lazy and dumb isn't just good career advice: it's the key to running a successful software business, too.
The only way to build great software is to make things as easy as possible for your users.
key_insight: The paradox of great programming is that intellectual humility and strategic laziness outperform raw intelligence and hard work.
